docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text: The Tinko dashboard is your central hub for accessing all classroom activities.

docs.title: Accessing the Dashboard
docs.text:
Navigate to:

http://your-pi-ip-address:8000/

Or simply:

http://your-pi-ip-address:8000

docs.title: Dashboard Layout
docs.text: The dashboard displays all installed plugins as interactive cards.

docs.title: Header Bar
docs.text:
At the top of the page you'll find:

  • Logo/Title: Your school name (if configured) or "Tinko"
  • Navigation: Links to Dashboard, Admin, and Settings
  • Language Selector: Globe icon to switch between English and Romanian

docs.title: App Cards
docs.text:
Each installed plugin appears as a card containing:

Element Description Icon Visual identifier for the plugin Name The plugin display name Description Brief explanation of what the plugin does Open Button Click to launch the plugin interface

docs.title: Apps vs Plugins
docs.text:
Tinko uses two terms interchangeably:

  • Plugins (technical term): The software modules that extend functionality
  • Apps (teacher term): What you see and interact with on the dashboard

When we say "App" in the interface, we're referring to a Plugin. They're the same thing - just different terminology for different audiences.

docs.title: Launching an App
docs.text:
1. Find the app card you want to use
  2. Click the Open button or the card itself
  3. The app interface loads in your browser

docs.title: Returning to Dashboard
docs.text:
From any app, click:

  • The Tinko logo/title in the navigation bar
  • The Dashboard link in the top navigation
  • Your browser's back button

Background Activities

When you navigate away from an app, it continues running in the background. See Background Activities for details.

docs.title: Refreshing the Dashboard
docs.text:
Press F5 or click your browser's refresh button to:

  • See newly installed plugins
  • Update plugin status
  • Refresh after enabling/disabling plugins

docs.title: Plugin States
docs.text:
Plugins can be in different states:

State Icon Meaning Active None Plugin is running normally Running :material-play-circle: Plugin has an active session Error :material-alert-circle: Plugin encountered an error Disabled :material-block-helper: Plugin is disabled in admin

docs.title: Admin Panel Access
docs.text:
Click Admin in the top navigation to access:

  • Plugin management (enable/disable)
  • User management
  • Database records
  • System settings

Admin Credentials

Use the superuser account you created during installation. Default: admin / admin123 (change this!)

docs.title: Mobile-Friendly
docs.text:
The dashboard works on any device:

  • Desktop: Full layout with all features
  • Tablet: Optimized card layout
  • Phone: Single-column, touch-friendly

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text:
• F5: Refresh page
  • Alt+Left Arrow: Go back
  • Tab: Navigate between elements

docs.title: Bookmarking
docs.text:
Bookmark specific plugin URLs for quick access:

http://pi-ip:8000/plugins/edupi/activity_timer/
http://pi-ip:8000/plugins/edupi/noise_monitor/
http://pi-ip:8000/plugins/edupi/routines/
http://pi-ip:8000/plugins/edupi/touch_piano/

docs.title: Dashboard Not Loading
docs.text:
1. Check that Tinko is running: 
    sudo systemctl status tinko
  2. Verify the URL and port
  3. Check network connectivity

docs.title: Plugins Not Showing
docs.text:
1. Ensure plugins are enabled in Admin Panel
  2. Check that migrations are complete: 
    uv run python manage.py migrate
  3. Restart Tinko: 
    sudo systemctl restart tinko

docs.title: Slow Loading
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi 3B+ may be slower than Pi 4
  • Check system resources: htop
  • Ensure adequate power supply (2.5A+ for Pi 4)

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text: Now that Tinko is installed, let's get you set up and familiar with the basics.

docs.title: Accessing the Dashboard
docs.text:
Open a web browser and navigate to:

http://your-pi-ip-address:8000/

Replace your-pi-ip-address with your Raspberry Pi's IP address.

Finding Your Pi's IP Address

On the Raspberry Pi, run: 

hostname -I
This will display the IP address(es) of your Pi.

docs.title: Logging In
docs.text:
If you haven't created an admin user yet, run:

uv run python manage.py createsuperuser

Follow the prompts to create your account.

Then log in at:

http://your-pi-ip-address:8000/admin/

docs.title: Understanding the Dashboard
docs.text:
The main dashboard (/) shows all your installed apps. You'll see cards for:

  • Activity Timer
  • Noise Monitor
  • Routines
  • Touch Piano

Each card shows:

  • Plugin name
  • Icon
  • Brief description
  • Link to open the app

docs.title: Changing the Language
docs.text:
Tinko supports English and Romanian:

  1. Look for the globe icon :material-earth: in the top navigation bar
  2. Click it to open the language selector
  3. Choose your preferred language

The interface will update immediately with translated text.

docs.title: Configuring Global Settings
docs.text:
Personalize Tinko for your school:

  1. Click Settings in the top navigation
  2. You'll see tabs for:
  3. Global Settings: School name, logo, robot name
  4. Plugin Settings: Individual plugin configurations

docs.title: Setting Your School Name and Logo
docs.text:
1. Go to the Global Settings tab
  2. Enter your school name
  3. Upload your school logo (max 400x400px, will be auto-resized)
  4. Set your robot's name (default: "Tinko")
  5. Click Save

Your school name and logo will appear on the dashboard.

docs.title: Enabling Plugins
docs.text:
All plugins are installed by default, but you can enable/disable them:

  1. Go to Admin Panel (/admin/)
  2. Navigate to Plugins
  3. Toggle plugins on/off as needed

Disabled plugins won't show on the dashboard.

docs.title: Your First Activity
docs.text:
Let's try the Activity Timer:

  1. From the dashboard, click Activity Timer
  2. You'll see preset buttons:
  3. Minute of Silence: 60 seconds, calming blue
  4. Break Time: 10 minutes, green theme
  5. Activity: 30 minutes, amber theme
  6. Click Minute of Silence to test
  7. Click the Start button

Watch the RGB LED change colors as time counts down!

Hardware Required

For the LED to light up, you need an RGB LED connected to pins 17 (red), 27 (green), and 22 (blue).

docs.title: Noise Monitor
docs.text:
1. Click Noise Monitor on the dashboard
  2. Click Start Monitoring
  3. Make some noise - watch the LEDs change color

docs.title: Touch Piano
docs.text:
1. Click Touch Piano
  2. Click keys on screen or use keyboard (A, S, D, F, G, H)
  3. Connect conductive materials to GPIO pins to play physically

docs.title: Routines
docs.text:
1. Click Routines
  2. Try a pre-built routine like Hand Warming Exercise
  3. Click Play to start text-to-speech

docs.title: Understanding Background Activities
docs.text:
When you start an activity and navigate away:

  • ✅ Continues running: Timer counts down, noise monitoring, GPIO outputs
  • ❌ Pauses: Web interface updates, WebSocket data streams
  • 🔄 Resumes: When you return, the page shows current status

This means you can:

  1. Start a 30-minute activity timer
  2. Switch to Noise Monitor to check classroom levels
  3. Return to timer to see remaining time
  4. All physical LED feedback continues throughout

Learn more: Background Activities

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide will walk you through installing Tinko on your Raspberry Pi.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing Tinko, ensure you have:

  • Raspberry Pi 4 (2GB+ RAM) or Raspberry Pi 3B+
  • MicroSD card (32GB+ recommended) with Raspberry Pi OS
  • Internet connection (for initial setup)
  • USB microphone or microphone module (for Noise Monitor)
  • Basic GPIO components (LEDs, resistors, breadboard) - see Hardware Requirements

docs.title: Step 1: Install UV Package Manager
docs.text:
Tinko uses UV for Python package management. Install it with:

curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

Restart your terminal or run:

source $HOME/.local/bin/env

docs.title: Step 2: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/rudolf-erdei/edu-pi.git
cd edu-pi

docs.title: Step 3: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
Install the base dependencies:

uv sync

If you're installing on an actual Raspberry Pi (not for development), also install GPIO-specific dependencies:

uv sync --extra pi

This installs RPi.GPIO and lgpio which are required for hardware control.

docs.title: Step 4: Configure Environment
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the project root:

nano .env

Add the following configuration:

DEBUG=False
SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key-here-change-this-in-production
ALLOWED_HOSTS=localhost,127.0.0.1,0.0.0.0,your-pi-ip-address
TIME_ZONE=Europe/Bucharest

Replace your-secret-key-here with a random string and update TIME_ZONE to your location.

docs.title: Step 5: Run Migrations
docs.text:
Set up the database:

uv run python manage.py migrate

docs.title: Step 6: Create Admin User
docs.text:
Create a superuser account:

uv run python manage.py createsuperuser

Follow the prompts to set username, email, and password.

Default credentials: - Username: admin - Password: admin123 (change this!)

docs.title: Step 7: Collect Static Files
docs.text: uv run python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

docs.title: Step 8: Start the Server
docs.text:
For development:

uv run python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000

For production (see Production Deployment):

uv run daphne -b 0.0.0.0 -p 8000 config.asgi:application

docs.title: Step 9: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Open a browser and navigate to:

  • Dashboard: http://your-pi-ip:8000/
  • Admin Panel: http://your-pi-ip:8000/admin/

You should see the Tinko dashboard!

docs.title: Production Deployment
docs.text: For a production environment where Tinko runs automatically on boot:

docs.title: Create Systemd Service
docs.text:
Create a service file:

sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/tinko.service

Add the following:

[Unit]
Description=Tinko Educational Platform
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=pi
WorkingDirectory=/home/pi/edu-pi
Environment="PATH=/home/pi/.local/bin"
Environment="PYTHONPATH=/home/pi/edu-pi"
Environment="DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=config.settings"
Environment="EDUPI_DEBUG=False"
ExecStartPre=/home/pi/.cargo/bin/uv run python manage.py migrate --noinput
ExecStartPre=/home/pi/.cargo/bin/uv run python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
ExecStart=/home/pi/.cargo/bin/uv run daphne -b 0.0.0.0 -p 8000 config.asgi:application
Restart=always
RestartSec=3

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

docs.title: Enable and Start Service
docs.text:
# Reload systemd
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

# Enable auto-start on boot
sudo systemctl enable tinko

# Start the service
sudo systemctl start tinko

# Check status
sudo systemctl status tinko

docs.title: View Logs
docs.text: sudo journalctl -u tinko -f

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
If you get "Address already in use":

# Find process using port 8000
sudo lsof -i :8000

# Kill the process
sudo kill -9 <PID>

docs.title: Permission Denied (GPIO)
docs.text:
If you get GPIO permission errors:

# Add user to gpio group
sudo usermod -a -G gpio pi

# Log out and back in for changes to take effect

docs.title: WebSocket Not Working
docs.text: Ensure you're using daphne or runserver (not WSGI). WebSocket requires ASGI server.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that Tinko is installed:

  1. First Steps - Create your admin account
  2. Hardware Setup - Connect LEDs and sensors
  3. Dashboard - Learn the interface

docs.title: Hardware Configuration
docs.text:
Before using plugins that require hardware (all built-in plugins), connect the necessary components:

  • Activity Timer: RGB LED (pins 17, 27, 22), Buzzer (pin 24)
  • Noise Monitor: 2x RGB LEDs (pins 5, 6, 13 and 19, 26, 16), USB microphone
  • Routines: Speaker or headphones (3.5mm jack), USB presenter (optional)
  • Touch Piano: 6 touch sensors (pins 23, 24, 10, 9, 25, 11), speaker

See Hardware Requirements for detailed wiring instructions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Welcome to the Tinko Teacher Guide! This section will help you understand what Tinko is, how it can enhance your classroom, and how to get started.

docs.title: What is Tinko?
docs.text: Tinko is an educational platform that runs on a Raspberry Pi computer, designed specifically for interactive classroom activities. It combines physical hardware (LEDs, sensors, touch pads) with a web-based dashboard that you control from any device on your network.

docs.title: Engage Students with Physical Interaction
docs.text:
Traditional classroom management often relies on verbal instructions. Tinko adds a visual, interactive element that captures students' attention:

  • Noise Monitor: Students see immediate visual feedback (colored LEDs) when the classroom gets too loud
  • Touch Piano: Students learn about circuits by touching conductive materials (bananas, foil, copper tape)
  • Activity Timer: Visual countdown with LED progress bar keeps students aware of time remaining
  • Routines: Text-to-speech guidance for warm-ups and transitions

docs.title: Easy to Use
docs.text:
You don't need to be technical to use Tinko:

  • Simple web interface accessible from any device
  • One-click activation of activities
  • Automatic background operation - activities continue even when you switch pages
  • Clear visual indicators and status messages

docs.title: Customizable
docs.text:
Make Tinko your own:

  • Set your school name and upload your logo
  • Customize the robot's name
  • Configure plugin settings to match your classroom needs
  • Support for multiple languages (English and Romanian)

docs.title: Transition Management
docs.text:
Use the Activity Timer to manage transitions between activities:

  • Set a 2-minute timer for cleanup time
  • LED shows green (plenty of time), then yellow (hurry up), then red (time's up)
  • Students learn to manage their time visually

docs.title: Classroom Noise Control
docs.text:
Activate the Noise Monitor during group work:

  • LED changes from green to yellow to red based on noise level
  • Students self-regulate when they see the color change
  • Dual LEDs show both instant noise and session average

docs.title: Sensory Breaks
docs.text:
Use the Touch Piano for sensory activities:

  • Students touch bananas or foil connected to GPIO pins
  • Each touch plays a musical note
  • Combines music, electronics, and tactile learning

docs.title: Daily Routines
docs.text:
Automate your daily routines with the Routines plugin:

  • Play hand-warming exercises before writing
  • Guide breathing exercises for calming transitions
  • USB presenter control allows you to advance from anywhere in the room

docs.title: Apps vs Plugins
docs.text:
Tinko uses two terms for the same thing:

  • Plugins (technical term): Software modules that extend functionality
  • Apps (teacher term): What you see on your dashboard

When you open the dashboard, you'll see cards for each "App" - these are the plugins you can activate.

docs.title: The Dashboard
docs.text:
The main dashboard at http://localhost:8000/ shows:

  • All installed apps with icons
  • Click any app to open it
  • Language selector in the top navigation
  • Settings link for configuration

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready to get started?

  1. Installation - Install Tinko on your Raspberry Pi
  2. First Steps - Create your admin account and explore
  3. Dashboard - Learn to navigate the interface
  4. Settings - Personalize Tinko for your school

Or jump to specific plugin guides:

  • Activity Timer
  • Noise Monitor
  • Routines
  • Touch Piano

docs.title: Settings
docs.text: The Settings page provides centralized configuration for Tinko and all plugins.

docs.title: Accessing Settings
docs.text:
Click the Settings link in the top navigation bar, or navigate to:

http://your-pi-ip-address:8000/settings/

docs.title: Settings Structure
docs.text:
Settings are organized into tabs:

  1. Global Settings - System-wide configuration
  2. Plugin Settings - Individual plugin configurations

docs.title: Global Settings
docs.text: Personalize Tinko for your school:

docs.title: School Name
docs.text:
Enter your school's name. This appears:

  • In the browser tab title
  • On the dashboard header
  • In emails (if email is configured)

docs.title: School Logo
docs.text:
Upload your school logo:

  • Supported formats: PNG, JPG, JPEG
  • Recommended size: 400x400 pixels or less
  • Auto-resized to max 400x400px with 200x200px thumbnail
  • Transparent backgrounds supported (PNG)

To upload:

  1. Click Choose File
  2. Select your logo image
  3. Click Save

The logo will appear on the dashboard and in the header.

docs.title: Robot Name
docs.text:
Customize what students call the system:

  • Default: "Tinko"
  • Examples: "Robo-Teacher", "Classroom Bot", "Sparky"

This name appears in: - Status messages - Plugin interfaces - Voice announcements (if TTS is enabled)

docs.title: Plugin Settings
docs.text: Each plugin can have its own configuration section.

docs.title: Accessing Plugin Settings
docs.text:
1. Click the Settings tab for the plugin you want to configure
  2. Modify the settings
  3. Click Save

docs.title: Common Setting Types
docs.text: Setting Type Description Example Text Single-line text input School name Number Numeric value with min/max LED brightness (10-100) Select Dropdown menu TTS engine selection Boolean Checkbox (on/off) Enable/disable feature File File upload Audio file, logo image

docs.title: Setting Namespaces
docs.text:
Settings use dot notation for organization:

  • Global: tinko.global.school_name
  • Plugin: edupi.activity_timer.default_duration

docs.title: Activity Timer Settings
docs.text:
• Default Duration: Default timer length in minutes (1-120)
  • LED Brightness: LED intensity percentage (10-100)
  • Warning Threshold: When LED changes color (5-50% remaining)

docs.title: Noise Monitor Settings
docs.text:
• Instant Window: Seconds for instant average calculation (5-60)
  • Session Window: Minutes for session average calculation (1-30)
  • LED Brightness: LED intensity percentage (10-100)
  • Enable Monitoring: Turn monitoring on/off

docs.title: Routines Settings
docs.text:
• Default TTS Engine: pyttsx3, edge-tts, or gTTS
  • Default TTS Speed: Speech rate (0.5x - 2.0x)
  • Presenter Mappings: Configure USB presenter buttons

docs.title: Touch Piano Settings
docs.text:
• Volume: Audio output level (0-100%)
  • Audio Device: ALSA device selection
  • Sensitivity: Touch detection sensitivity (1-10)

docs.title: Settings Hierarchy
docs.text:
Settings are stored with priorities:

  1. User Settings (if implemented)
  2. Plugin Settings (plugin-specific)
  3. Global Settings (system-wide defaults)

When a setting is not defined at a higher level, it falls back to the next level.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
• Use clear, descriptive names
  • Avoid special characters
  • Keep school names under 50 characters

docs.title: Image Uploads
docs.text:
• Use high-quality logos (at least 200x200px)
  • Optimize file size (< 500KB)
  • Use PNG for transparency support

docs.title: Configuration Backup
docs.text:
Settings are stored in the database. Back up regularly:

# Backup SQLite database
cp db.sqlite3 db.sqlite3.backup

# Or use Django's dumpdata
uv run python manage.py dumpdata core.plugin_system > settings_backup.json

docs.title: Settings Not Saving
docs.text:
1. Check database migrations: 
    uv run python manage.py migrate
  2. Verify file permissions on the database
  3. Check disk space

docs.title: Logo Not Displaying
docs.text:
1. Ensure migrations are complete
  2. Check that MEDIA_ROOT is configured
  3. Verify the image format (PNG/JPG only)

docs.title: Settings Changes Not Applied
docs.text:
Some settings require a restart:

sudo systemctl restart tinko

docs.title: Image Upload Fails
docs.text:
• Check file size (max usually 5-10MB)
  • Verify image dimensions
  • Ensure write permissions on media directory

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Some settings can be overridden via environment variables:

DEBUG=False
SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key
TIME_ZONE=Europe/Bucharest

See Configuration Reference for details.

docs.title: Database Settings
docs.text:
Settings are stored in:

  • SQLite (default): db.sqlite3 file
  • PostgreSQL (production): Requires configuration in config/settings.py

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Dashboard - Return to the main interface
  • Plugin guides - Configure individual plugins
  • Background Activities - Understand independent operation

docs.title: Activity Timer
docs.text: Visual countdown timer for classroom activities with configurable preset profiles and RGB LED feedback.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Activity Timer helps manage classroom time with visual countdown displays and LED indicators. Perfect for activity transitions, breaks, and timed exercises.

URL: /plugins/edupi/activity_timer/

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• RGB LED (common cathode)
  • 3x 220Ω resistors
  • Optional: Buzzer module
  • Breadboard and jumper wires

docs.title: GPIO Pin Connections
docs.text: Component GPIO Pin Physical Pin Color LED Red 17 11 Red wire LED Green 27 13 Green wire LED Blue 22 15 Blue wire Buzzer (optional) 24 18 Any wire

docs.title: Preset Profiles
docs.text: Four built-in presets for common classroom scenarios:

docs.title: Minute of Silence
docs.text:
• Duration: 60 seconds
  • Display Color: Indigo (calming)
  • LED Colors: Blue tones
  • Use Case: Calming students before activities

docs.title: Break Time
docs.text:
• Duration: 10 minutes
  • Display Color: Green
  • LED Colors: Green to yellow to red
  • Use Case: Standard break periods

docs.title: Activity
docs.text:
• Duration: 30 minutes
  • Display Color: Amber
  • LED Colors: Amber to orange to red
  • Use Case: Regular class activities

docs.title: Custom
docs.text:
• Duration: User-defined (1-120 minutes)
  • Display Color: User-selected
  • LED Colors: Configurable
  • Use Case: Any custom timing need

docs.title: Main Timer Display
docs.text: Large digital countdown showing: - Remaining Time: Minutes and seconds - Status: Running, Paused, or Stopped - Progress: Visual indicator

docs.title: Preset Buttons
docs.text:
Click any preset to load its configuration:

  1. Minute of Silence - Blue button
  2. Break Time - Green button
  3. Activity - Amber button
  4. Custom - Opens duration selector

docs.title: Playback Controls
docs.text: Button Action Keyboard Shortcut Start Begin countdown Space Pause Pause timer Space Resume Continue countdown Space Stop End timer, reset to 0 Escape

docs.title: LED Visual Feedback
docs.text:
The RGB LED shows remaining time visually:

Time Remaining LED Color Meaning > 50% Green Plenty of time 20-50% Yellow Time passing < 20% Red Time running out Finished Flashing Red Time's up!

LED Brightness

Adjust LED brightness in Settings to match your classroom lighting.

docs.title: Custom Timer
docs.text:
Create timers with custom durations:

  1. Click Custom preset button
  2. Enter duration (1-120 minutes)
  3. Select display color
  4. Click Start

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text:
Access settings at /plugins/edupi/activity_timer/config/ or via the main Settings page:

  • Default Duration: Default timer length (1-120 min)
  • LED Brightness: LED intensity percentage (10-100%)
  • Warning Threshold: When LED changes color (5-50% remaining)
  • Enable Buzzer: Play sound when timer completes

docs.title: Session Tracking
docs.text:
Each timer session is recorded in the database:

  • Start time
  • Duration
  • Preset used
  • Completion status

View history in the plugin interface.

docs.title: Transition Management
docs.text:
Set a 2-minute timer for cleanup: 1. Select Custom preset 2. Set duration to 2 minutes 3. Choose calming blue color 4. Click Start

Students see the LED change from green → yellow → red, helping them manage time.

docs.title: Focus Sessions
docs.text: Use Minute of Silence before writing activities: - Calming blue display - 60-second countdown - Students prepare mentally while watching the timer

docs.title: Activity Timing
docs.text: Standard 30-minute activity: 1. Select Activity preset 2. Click Start 3. LED shows progress throughout 4. Optional buzzer alerts at end

docs.title: LED Brightness
docs.text:
• Classroom with natural light: 80-100%
  • Dim classroom: 50-70%
  • Dark room: 30-50%

docs.title: Warning Threshold
docs.text:
• Younger students: 30% (earlier warning)
  • Older students: 20% (standard)
  • Urgent activities: 10% (minimal warning)

docs.title: Combining with Other Plugins
docs.text:
The Activity Timer runs independently:

  1. Start Activity Timer
  2. Navigate to Noise Monitor
  3. Check classroom noise while timer runs
  4. Return to see remaining time

See Background Activities for details.

docs.title: LED Not Lighting
docs.text:
1. Check wiring connections
  2. Verify GPIO pins in plugin settings
  3. Check LED brightness setting (> 0%)
  4. Ensure LED is common cathode

docs.title: Buzzer Not Working
docs.text:
1. Verify buzzer polarity (+/-)
  2. Check buzzer enabled in settings
  3. Test with multimeter

docs.title: Timer Not Starting
docs.text:
1. Check browser console for JavaScript errors
  2. Verify WebSocket connection (if applicable)
  3. Refresh page and try again

docs.title: Wrong Colors
docs.text:
• Check RGB LED wiring matches pin assignments
  • Common cathode LEDs have different wiring than common anode

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• Updates every second during countdown
  • Uses threading for background operation
  • Stores session data in SQLite database
  • HTTP polling for web interface updates
  • WebSocket support planned for future versions

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Dashboard - Navigate between plugins
  • Settings - Configure plugin options
  • Background Activities - Independent operation
  • GPIO Wiring - Hardware setup guide

docs.title: LCD Display Plugin
docs.text: The LCD Display plugin provides a visual "face" for your Tinko robot using a 2.8" TFT LCD screen. The robot displays animated mood-based faces that respond to classroom conditions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The LCD Display turns your Raspberry Pi into an expressive robot with: - 6 Different Moods: Happy, Neutral, Sad, Angry, Laughing, and Concentrated - Animated Faces: Each mood alternates between 2 face variations (5s + 2s cycle) - Web Control: Change moods instantly via the web interface - Auto-Animation: Faces animate continuously when not displaying content - SPI Interface: Hardware-accelerated display updates

docs.title: Display Module
docs.text:
• Size: 2.8" TFT LCD
  • Resolution: 320x240 pixels
  • Driver: ILI9341
  • Interface: SPI (4-wire)
  • Touch: Not required (optional)

docs.title: Wiring Configuration
docs.text:
CRITICAL: CS Pin Must Be Pin 15 (GPIO 22)

LCD Pin RPi Physical Pin RPi GPIO Function VCC Pin 1 or 17 - 3.3V Power GND Pin 6 or 9 - Ground CS Pin 15 GPIO 22 Chip Select RST Pin 16 GPIO 23 Reset DC Pin 18 GPIO 24 Data/Command MOSI Pin 19 GPIO 10 SPI Data In SCK Pin 23 GPIO 11 SPI Clock LED/BL Pin 12 GPIO 18 Backlight (PWM) MISO Pin 21 GPIO 9 Data Out (optional)

docs.title: Setup Steps
docs.text:
1. Connect wires according to the table above
  2. Enable SPI interface: 
    sudo raspi-config
# Interface Options → SPI → Enable
  3. Add permissions: 
    sudo usermod -a -G spi,gpio $USER
sudo usermod -a -G spi,gpio www-data
  4. Reboot the Raspberry Pi

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
Install required libraries:

uv add adafruit-circuitpython-rgb-display adafruit-blinka

Or during installation: 

uv sync --extra pi

docs.title: 1. Happy (Default)
docs.text: Faces: :) and :D - Default mood when system starts - Cheerful expression with animated smile - Use: General operation, welcoming students

docs.title: 2. Neutral
docs.text: Faces: :| and blink - Straight face for minor misbehavior - Expressionless, professional look - Use: First warning to students

docs.title: 3. Sad
docs.text: Faces: :( and sadder - Droopy eyes and downward mouth - Indicates disappointment - Use: Continued misbehavior

docs.title: 4. Angry
docs.text: Faces: >:| and furious - Slanted eyebrows, tight mouth - Serious expression - Use: Serious discipline issues

docs.title: 5. Laughing
docs.text: Faces: :D and big laugh - Curved eyes, open laughing mouth - Joyful expression - Use: Celebrations, fun activities

docs.title: 6. Concentrated
docs.text: Faces: Focused and thinking - Narrowed eyes, small thoughtful mouth - Studious expression - Use: Learning activities, focused work

docs.title: Web Interface
docs.text:
Access the LCD Display control panel: 

http://[raspberry-pi-ip]:8000/plugins/edupi/lcd_display/

Controls: - Mood Buttons: Click any mood to change the robot's expression - Brightness Slider: Adjust backlight (0-100%) - Show Smiley: Resume animation after displaying text - Clear Screen: Turn off display - Display Text: Show custom message (pauses animation)

docs.title: API Endpoints
docs.text:
Set Mood: 

curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/plugins/edupi/lcd_display/api/set-mood/ \
  -d "mood=sad"

Get Current Mood: 

curl http://localhost:8000/plugins/edupi/lcd_display/api/get-mood/

Available moods: happy, neutral, sad, angry, laughing, concentrated

docs.title: Programmatic Usage
docs.text:
from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.lcd_service import lcd_service
from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.mood import Mood

# Set mood
lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.SAD)

# Or by name
lcd_service.set_mood_by_name("angry")

# Check if misbehaving
if lcd_service.is_misbehaving():
    print("Classroom is misbehaving!")

# Display text (pauses animation)
lcd_service.show_text("Time to focus!")

# Resume animation
lcd_service.resume_face_animation()

docs.title: Face Cycle
docs.text:
Each mood displays 2 faces that alternate: 1. Main Face (5 seconds) - Primary expression 2. Variation (2 seconds) - Secondary expression (blink, bigger smile, etc.)

Total cycle: 7 seconds

docs.title: Automatic Behaviors
docs.text:
• Startup: Shows Happy mood with animation
  • Text Display: Pauses animation, shows text
  • Countdown: Pauses animation, shows countdown
  • Resume: Click "Show Smiley" to restart animation

docs.title: Calling Plugins from Other Plugins
docs.text: All plugins can call the LCD Display plugin to control the robot's mood and display. This is done by importing the LCD service singleton.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.lcd_service import lcd_service
from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.mood import Mood

# Always check if LCD is initialized before using
if lcd_service.is_initialized():
    # Set mood by enum
    lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.HAPPY)

    # Or by name
    lcd_service.set_mood_by_name("concentrated")

    # Display custom text
    lcd_service.show_text("Time's up!")

    # Adjust backlight
    lcd_service.set_backlight(50)  # 50% brightness

docs.title: Complete Example: Noise-Based Mood Changes
docs.text:
Here's how to change the robot's mood based on classroom noise levels:

from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.lcd_service import lcd_service
from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.mood import Mood

def update_robot_mood(noise_level):
    """Update LCD mood based on noise level.

    Args:
        noise_level: Current noise level (0-100)
    """
    if not lcd_service.is_initialized():
        return

    if noise_level > 80:
        # Very noisy - robot is angry
        lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.ANGRY)
    elif noise_level > 60:
        # Quite noisy - robot is sad
        lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.SAD)
    elif noise_level > 40:
        # Moderate noise - robot is neutral
        lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.NEUTRAL)
    elif noise_level > 20:
        # Quiet - robot is happy
        lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.HAPPY)
    else:
        # Very quiet - robot is concentrated
        lcd_service.set_mood(Mood.CONCENTRATED)

docs.title: Available Methods
docs.text:
Mood Control: - lcd_service.set_mood(mood: Mood) - Set mood using Mood enum - lcd_service.set_mood_by_name(name: str) - Set mood by string name - lcd_service.get_current_mood() - Get current mood - lcd_service.get_available_moods() - List all available moods - lcd_service.is_misbehaving() - Check if mood indicates misbehavior

Display Control: - lcd_service.show_text(text, position, font_size) - Display text - lcd_service.clear_screen() - Clear display - lcd_service.set_backlight(brightness) - Set backlight (0-100)

Animation: - lcd_service.start_face_animation() - Start mood animation - lcd_service.stop_face_animation() - Stop animation - lcd_service.pause_face_animation() - Pause animation - lcd_service.resume_face_animation() - Resume animation

docs.title: Important Considerations
docs.text:
1. Check Initialization: Always call lcd_service.is_initialized() before using
  2. SPI Conflict: Touch Piano cannot be used simultaneously with LCD due to SPI conflicts
  3. Dependencies: All plugins automatically declare LCD Display as a dependency
  4. Thread Safety: LCD service is thread-safe for concurrent access

docs.title: Activity Timer
docs.text: When countdown is running: - LCD pauses mood animation - Shows countdown timer - Returns to mood animation after completion

docs.title: Noise Monitor
docs.text: Can trigger mood changes based on noise levels - see example above.

docs.title: Touch Piano
docs.text: Cannot use simultaneously with LCD Display (SPI conflict). Disable Touch Piano plugin when using LCD.

docs.title: Display Not Working
docs.text:
1. Check SPI enabled: 

    ls /dev/spi*
# Should show /dev/spidev0.0 and /dev/spidev0.1

  2. Verify CS pin location:

  3. Must be on Pin 15 (GPIO 22), NOT Pin 24

  4. Check permissions: 

    groups $USER
# Should include: gpio, spi

  5. Test display: 

    uv run python -c "
from plugins.edupi.lcd_display.lcd_service import lcd_service
lcd_service.initialize()
lcd_service.show_smiley_face()
"

docs.title: Web Interface Shows "Disconnected"
docs.text:
• SPI interface not enabled
  • Wrong CS pin wiring
  • Missing permissions
  • Hardware connection issue

docs.title: Animation Not Working
docs.text:
• Check logs/lcd_display.log for errors
  • Ensure spidev library installed: uv sync --extra pi
  • Verify all dependencies installed

docs.title: Face Looks Wrong
docs.text:
• Different LCD module may have different orientation
  • Try changing rotation in settings (0, 90, 180, 270)
  • Check backlight is connected

docs.title: Libraries Used
docs.text:
• adafruit-circuitpython-rgb-display: ILI9341 driver
  • adafruit-blinka: CircuitPython compatibility
  • Pillow: Image drawing and manipulation
  • gpiozero: PWM backlight control

docs.title: SPI Configuration
docs.text:
• Port: 0
  • Device: 0
  • Baudrate: 16MHz
  • Mode: 0

docs.title: Display Specifications
docs.text:
• Resolution: 240x320 (portrait) or 320x240 (landscape)
  • Color Depth: 16-bit (65k colors)
  • Refresh Rate: ~60Hz
  • Interface: 4-wire SPI

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Face update: ~50ms
  • Animation smooth: 7-second cycle
  • Text display: Instant
  • Backlight PWM: 0-100%

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Wire carefully: CS pin must be on Pin 15
  2. Test first: Run initialization test before full setup
  3. Check conflicts: Disable Touch Piano when using LCD
  4. Monitor logs: Check logs/lcd_display.log for issues
  5. Use moods: Set appropriate moods for classroom management

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
• 3.3V only: Never apply 5V to display pins
  • Current: Backlight draws ~100mA at full brightness
  • Heat: Display may get warm during operation
  • Mounting: Secure display to prevent student contact with pins

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Hardware Requirements - Complete component list
  • GPIO Pin Assignments - Detailed pin mapping
  • Plugin API - Create custom integrations

docs.title: Support
docs.text: For issues with the LCD Display: 1. Check troubleshooting section above 2. Review logs in logs/lcd_display.log 3. Verify wiring against pin table 4. Test with simple initialization script

docs.title: Noise Monitor
docs.text: Real-time classroom noise visualization with dual RGB LED feedback and WebSocket-based updates.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Noise Monitor helps students self-regulate their volume levels by providing continuous visual feedback through RGB LEDs. Perfect for group work and maintaining appropriate classroom noise levels.

URL: /plugins/edupi/noise_monitor/

WebSocket: ws://localhost:8000/ws/noise-monitor/

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• 2x RGB LEDs (common cathode)
  • 6x 220Ω resistors
  • USB microphone or microphone module
  • Breadboard and jumper wires

docs.title: GPIO Pin Connections
docs.text:
LED 1 (Instant Noise - 10-second average):

Component GPIO Pin Physical Pin Color LED 1 Red 5 29 Red wire LED 1 Green 6 31 Green wire LED 1 Blue 13 33 Blue wire

LED 2 (Session Average - 5-10 minute average):

Component GPIO Pin Physical Pin Color LED 2 Red 19 35 Red wire LED 2 Green 26 37 Green wire LED 2 Blue 16 36 Blue wire

docs.title: Dual LED System
docs.text: The Noise Monitor uses two separate RGB LEDs with different purposes:

docs.title: LED 1: Instant Noise
docs.text:
Shows the current noise level over a short time window:

  • Time Window: Configurable (default: 10 seconds, range: 5-60s)
  • Purpose: Immediate feedback on current noise
  • Use Case: Students get instant feedback when they get too loud

docs.title: LED 2: Session Average
docs.text:
Shows the overall noise level for the session:

  • Time Window: Configurable (default: 5 minutes, range: 1-30min)
  • Purpose: Track overall session noise quality
  • Use Case: Reward quiet sessions, identify problematic periods

docs.title: Noise Profiles
docs.text: Choose from four preset noise profiles or create custom thresholds:

docs.title: Test Profile
docs.text:
• Yellow Threshold: 30 dB
  • Red Threshold: 50 dB
  • Use Case: Silent mode, testing

docs.title: Teaching Profile
docs.text:
• Yellow Threshold: 40 dB
  • Red Threshold: 70 dB
  • Use Case: Normal classroom teaching

docs.title: Group Work Profile
docs.text:
• Yellow Threshold: 50 dB
  • Red Threshold: 80 dB
  • Use Case: Collaborative activities, discussions

docs.title: Custom Profile
docs.text:
• Yellow Threshold: User-defined
  • Red Threshold: User-defined
  • Use Case: Specific classroom acoustics

docs.title: LED Color Coding
docs.text:
Both LEDs use the same color scheme:

Noise Level LED Color Meaning Below Yellow Green Quiet/Silent Yellow Threshold Yellow Moderate noise Red Threshold Red Too loud

Visual Feedback

Green = Good, Yellow = Caution, Red = Too Loud

docs.title: Main Display
docs.text:
• Instant Noise Bar: Visual representation of current noise (0-100%)
  • Session Average Bar: Visual representation of session average
  • Digital Readings: Numeric dB values
  • LED Indicators: On-screen LEDs matching physical LEDs

docs.title: Historical Data
docs.text:
View recent noise readings:

  • Last 50 readings displayed
  • Timestamp for each reading
  • Color-coded noise levels
  • Helps identify patterns

docs.title: Session Controls
docs.text: Button Action Start Monitoring Begin noise measurement Stop Monitoring Pause noise measurement Reset Session Clear session average data Profile Selector Choose noise profile

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Access settings at /plugins/edupi/noise_monitor/config/:

docs.title: Time Windows
docs.text:
• Instant Window: Seconds for instant average (5-60s)
  • Session Window: Minutes for session average (1-30min)

docs.title: LED Settings
docs.text:
• LED Brightness: Intensity percentage (10-100%)
  • Enable Monitoring: Turn monitoring on/off

docs.title: Thresholds
docs.text:
Configure custom profile thresholds:

  • Yellow Threshold: Moderate noise level
  • Red Threshold: High noise level

docs.title: Real-Time Updates
docs.text:
The Noise Monitor uses WebSocket for real-time updates:

  • Update Frequency: 10 times per second
  • Latency: < 100ms
  • Auto-reconnect: Reconnects if connection drops
  • Fallback: HTTP polling if WebSocket unavailable

WebSocket Connection

The browser automatically connects to WebSocket when the page loads. Look for the connection status indicator.

docs.title: Group Work Monitoring
docs.text:
1. Select Group Work profile
  2. Click Start Monitoring
  3. Place LED where students can see it
  4. Students self-regulate when LED turns yellow/red

docs.title: Silent Reading Time
docs.text:
1. Select Test profile (lowest thresholds)
  2. Start monitoring
  3. LED provides instant feedback
  4. Session average shows overall compliance

docs.title: Transition Management
docs.text:
1. Monitor noise during transitions
  2. Session average shows if transitions are getting quieter over time
  3. Use data to encourage quieter transitions

docs.title: Classroom Acoustics Testing
docs.text:
1. Use Custom profile
  2. Set thresholds based on your room
  3. Test different times of day
  4. Find optimal thresholds for your space

docs.title: LED Placement
docs.text:
• Place LEDs where students can easily see them
  • Consider using diffusers or covers for softer light
  • Avoid direct eye contact with bright LEDs
  • Position at student eye level when possible

docs.title: Profile Selection
docs.text:
• Start of class: Teaching profile
  • Group work: Group Work profile
  • Tests/Silent work: Test profile
  • Experiment: Custom profile for your room

docs.title: Session Management
docs.text:
• Reset session at the start of each class period
  • Keep session window consistent for fair comparison
  • Review historical data to identify patterns

docs.title: Calibration
docs.text:
1. Set all thresholds in a quiet room
  2. Note the baseline reading
  3. Set yellow threshold 10-20% above baseline
  4. Set red threshold at unacceptable level

docs.title: LEDs Not Responding
docs.text:
1. Check all 6 wire connections
  2. Verify RGB LED common cathode configuration
  3. Check LED brightness setting (> 0%)
  4. Ensure monitoring is enabled

docs.title: No Noise Readings
docs.text:
1. Check USB microphone connection
  2. Verify microphone is not muted
  3. Check system audio settings
  4. Test microphone with: arecord -l

docs.title: WebSocket Disconnected
docs.text:
1. Check browser console for errors
  2. Verify WebSocket URL is correct
  3. Refresh page to reconnect
  4. Check if firewall blocks port 8000

docs.title: LEDs Show Wrong Colors
docs.text:
• Common cathode vs common anode confusion
  • Check wiring matches pin assignments
  • Verify profile thresholds are reasonable

docs.title: Noisy Baseline
docs.text:
• Adjust thresholds upward
  • Check for environmental noise sources
  • Consider acoustic treatment
  • Use custom profile with higher thresholds

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• Audio Sampling: 10 times per second
  • Averaging: Exponential moving average
  • WebSocket Protocol: Django Channels
  • Database: Stores last 1000 readings
  • GPIO Control: PWM for smooth color transitions

docs.title: Data Privacy
docs.text:
• Noise data stays local on Raspberry Pi
  • No cloud storage
  • Historical data auto-deletes after 1000 readings
  • Can be configured to store less data

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Background Activities - Runs when you navigate away
  • Settings - Configure plugin options
  • GPIO Wiring - Hardware setup guide
  • WebSocket - Technical implementation

docs.title: Routines
docs.text: Text-to-speech classroom routines with USB presenter support for warm-ups, transitions, and cooldowns.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Routines plugin provides guided classroom activities with synchronized text highlighting and multiple TTS engine support. Perfect for hand-warming exercises, calming transitions, and instructional sequences.

URL: /plugins/edupi/routines/

WebSocket: ws://localhost:8000/ws/routines/

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• Speaker or headphones (3.5mm jack or USB)
  • USB wireless presenter (optional but recommended)
  • Internet connection (for online TTS engines)

docs.title: Multi-Engine TTS Support
docs.text:
Three text-to-speech engines available:

Engine Type Quality Requirements pyttsx3 Offline Good None edge-tts Online Excellent Internet gTTS Online Good Internet

Recommendation: Use pyttsx3 for offline reliability, edge-tts when internet is available.

docs.title: Audio File Upload
docs.text:
Override TTS with custom audio files:

  • Supported Formats: MP3, WAV
  • Use Cases: Music, custom recordings, better pronunciations
  • Fallback: Automatically uses TTS if no file uploaded

docs.title: USB Presenter Support
docs.text:
Control routines wirelessly with standard USB presenters (Logitech, Kensington, etc.):

Button Mappings:

Presenter Button Action Default Mapping Next Advance line Next button Previous Go back Previous button Play/Pause Pause/resume Play button Stop End routine Black screen button

Presenter Detection

Plug in the USB presenter and it's automatically detected. No drivers needed on Linux systems.

docs.title: Hand Warming Exercise
docs.text:
Prepares students for writing activities:

"Let's warm up our hands for writing!
Rub your palms together, rub, rub, rub.
Now your fingers, wiggle them up high.
Shake them out, shake, shake, shake.
Ready to write, nice and warm!"

docs.title: Finger Stretch
docs.text:
Hand stretching routine:

"Let's stretch our fingers!
Open wide, close tight.
Wiggle left, wiggle right.
Roll your wrists around and around.
Stretch complete, hands ready!"

docs.title: Deep Breathing
docs.text:
Calming transition routine:

"Take a deep breath in... hold... and out.
Breathe in peace... hold... breathe out stress.
Your body is calm, your mind is clear.
Ready to learn!"

docs.title: Step 1: Create Category
docs.text:
Organize routines into categories:

  1. Go to Routines dashboard
  2. Click Categories
  3. Click Add Category
  4. Enter name (e.g., "Warm-up", "Transition", "Cooldown")

docs.title: Step 2: Create Routine
docs.text:
1. Click New Routine
  2. Enter title
  3. Select category
  4. Add description (optional)
  5. Add content (one instruction per line)

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Audio
docs.text:
Option A: Use TTS - Select TTS engine (pyttsx3, edge-tts, gTTS) - Set speed (0.5x - 2.0x) - Select language

Option B: Upload Audio - Click Upload Audio - Select MP3 or WAV file - Audio will play instead of TTS

docs.title: Step 4: Save and Test
docs.text:
1. Click Save
  2. Click Test TTS to preview
  3. Adjust settings if needed

docs.title: Full-Screen Player
docs.text:
When you start a routine, it opens in full-screen mode with:

  • Current Line: Highlighted in large text
  • Previous/Next Lines: Smaller, dimmed text
  • Progress Indicator: Shows position in routine
  • Control Bar: Play/pause, previous, next, stop

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Key Action Space Play/Pause → Next line ← Previous line Escape Stop routine

docs.title: Visual Highlighting
docs.text:
Text highlighting synchronizes with speech:

  • Current line: Bold, bright color
  • Previous lines: Dimmed
  • Next lines: Grayed out
  • Auto-advance: Moves to next line automatically

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
Organize routines by purpose:

Category Purpose Examples Warm-up Prepare for activity Hand warming, stretching Transition Between activities Breathing, movement breaks Cooldown End of session Calming, reflection Custom Any purpose Teacher-defined

docs.title: WebSocket Synchronization
docs.text:
Real-time synchronization across all connected clients:

  • Line Updates: Current line synced instantly
  • Playback State: Play/pause synchronized
  • Progress: All see the same position
  • Control: Any client can control playback

Multi-Device Setup

Open the routine player on your phone while presenting on the main screen. Controls sync between devices!

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Access settings via Settings > Routines:

docs.title: Default TTS Engine
docs.text: Choose your preferred default engine: - pyttsx3 (offline, works everywhere) - edge-tts (high quality, requires internet) - gTTS (Google TTS, requires internet)

docs.title: Default TTS Speed
docs.text:
• Slow: 0.5x - 0.8x (for young learners)
  • Normal: 1.0x (standard)
  • Fast: 1.2x - 2.0x (for quick routines)

docs.title: Presenter Button Mappings
docs.text:
Customize which presenter buttons perform which actions:

  1. Go to Settings > Routines > Presenter Mapping
  2. Select button and assign action
  3. Click Save

docs.title: Routine Length
docs.text:
• Warm-ups: 30-60 seconds
  • Transitions: 15-30 seconds
  • Cooldowns: 30-120 seconds

Keep routines short for maximum engagement.

docs.title: Line Breaks
docs.text:
Place line breaks at natural pauses:

Good:
"Take a deep breath in...
hold...
and breathe out."

Less Good:
"Take a deep breath in... hold... and breathe out."

docs.title: TTS Speed Selection
docs.text:
• Young students (K-2): 0.8x speed
  • Standard (3-5): 1.0x speed
  • Quick instructions: 1.2x speed

docs.title: Presenter Usage
docs.text:
• Keep presenter in hand during routines
  • Walk around the room while presenting
  • Use visual cues along with audio
  • Test presenter buttons before class

docs.title: No Audio Output
docs.text:
1. Check speaker/headphone connection
  2. Verify volume is up
  3. Test with: speaker-test -t wav
  4. Check audio device in settings

docs.title: TTS Not Working
docs.text:
pyttsx3 issues: - Install espeak: sudo apt-get install espeak - Check espeak installation: espeak "test"

edge-tts/gTTS issues: - Verify internet connection - Check firewall settings - Try offline pyttsx3 as backup

docs.title: Presenter Not Detected
docs.text:
1. Unplug and re-plug USB receiver
  2. Check system recognizes device: lsusb
  3. Verify evdev library installed (Linux)
  4. Check button mappings in settings

docs.title: WebSocket Disconnected
docs.text:
• Refresh page to reconnect
  • Check network connection
  • Verify WebSocket URL
  • Look for firewall blocking

docs.title: Sync Issues
docs.text:
If text and audio get out of sync:

  1. Pause playback
  2. Click the correct line
  3. Resume playback
  4. Or stop and restart routine

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• Audio: pygame for playback
  • TTS: pyttsx3 (offline), edge-tts, gTTS (online)
  • Presenter: evdev library for USB input (Linux)
  • WebSocket: Django Channels
  • File Storage: Local filesystem in media/routines/

docs.title: Session Tracking
docs.text:
Each routine session is logged:

  • Routine name
  • Start/end time
  • Completion percentage
  • Audio engine used

docs.title: File Storage
docs.text:
• Audio files stored in media/routines/
  • Automatic file naming with timestamps
  • Can be backed up by copying directory

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Background Activities - Runs when you navigate away
  • Settings - Configure TTS and presenter
  • Dashboard - Access routines
  • WebSocket - Real-time sync technical details

docs.title: Touch Piano
docs.text: Interactive musical experience using conductive materials connected to GPIO pins. Learn about circuits while making music!

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Touch Piano transforms everyday conductive materials into musical keys. Students learn about circuits, conductivity, and music while having fun.

URL: /plugins/edupi/touch_piano/

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• 6 touch sensors or capacitive touch inputs
  • Conductive materials (bananas, foil, copper tape, etc.)
  • Speaker or headphones (3.5mm jack)
  • 6x 10kΩ pull-up resistors (if using bare wire)
  • Breadboard and jumper wires

docs.title: GPIO Pin Connections
docs.text:
6 Piano Keys:

Key Note GPIO Pin Physical Pin Wire Color Key 1 C (Do) 23 16 Red Key 2 D (Re) 24 18 Orange Key 3 E (Mi) 10 19 Yellow Key 4 F (Fa) 9 21 Green Key 5 G (Sol) 25 22 Blue Key 6 A (La) 11 23 Purple

docs.title: Capacitive Touch Sensing
docs.text:
The Touch Piano uses capacitive touch sensing to detect when conductive materials are touched:

  1. Circuit Setup: Conductive material connected to GPIO pin
  2. Pull-up Resistor: Maintains HIGH state when not touched
  3. Touch Detection: When touched, capacitance changes
  4. Signal Processing: GPIO detects the change and triggers note
  5. Audio Output: pygame synthesizes and plays the note

docs.title: Conductive Materials
docs.text:
Many everyday items work as piano keys:

Material Conductivity Best For Bananas Excellent Fun demonstrations Aluminum Foil Excellent Custom shapes Copper Tape Excellent Permanent installations Play-Doh Good Child-safe Water Moderate Science experiments Plants Moderate Living piano

Best Results

Moist materials (like fresh fruit) work better than dry materials.

docs.title: Piano Display
docs.text:
The web interface shows a 6-key piano:

  • Visual Keys: Click to play
  • Key Labels: Shows note names (C, D, E, F, G, A)
  • Status: Shows which key is currently pressed
  • Volume Control: Adjust output level

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text:
Play using your computer keyboard:

Computer Key Piano Key Note A Key 1 C S Key 2 D D Key 3 E F Key 4 F G Key 5 G H Key 6 A

docs.title: Option 1: Simple Wire Connection
docs.text:
1. Connect GPIO pins to conductive materials with wire
  2. Add 10kΩ pull-up resistors between each pin and 3.3V
  3. Connect common ground
  4. Touch material to play

docs.title: Option 2: Using Capacitive Touch Sensors (TTP223)
docs.text:
1. Connect TTP223 modules to GPIO pins
  2. Connect sensor outputs to materials
  3. No pull-up resistors needed
  4. More reliable touch detection

docs.title: Wiring Diagram
docs.text:
GPIO 23 (Pin 16) ----[10kΩ]---- 3.3V (Pin 1)
     |
     +---- Wire to banana/foil

Repeat for all 6 keys

Safety

Always use 3.3V power, not 5V. High voltage can damage GPIO pins.

docs.title: Science Concepts
docs.text:
• Conductivity: What materials conduct electricity?
  • Circuits: How do circuits work?
  • Resistance: Why do some materials work better?
  • Capacitance: How does touch sensing work?

docs.title: Music Concepts
docs.text:
• Pitch: Higher/lower notes
  • Octaves: Scale relationships
  • Melody: Creating simple tunes
  • Rhythm: Timing and beats

docs.title: Technology Concepts
docs.text:
• GPIO: General Purpose Input/Output
  • Digital Input: Reading on/off states
  • Audio Synthesis: Creating sounds programmatically
  • Human-Computer Interaction: Physical computing

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Access settings via Settings > Touch Piano:

docs.title: Volume
docs.text:
• Range: 0-100%
  • Default: 80%
  • Adjust based on classroom noise level

docs.title: Audio Device
docs.text:
• default: System default audio
  • hw:0,0: Hardware device 0
  • plughw:1,0: USB audio device

To list available devices:

aplay -l

docs.title: Sensitivity
docs.text:
• Range: 1-10
  • Default: 5
  • Higher = more sensitive touch detection
  • Increase if touches aren't registering
  • Decrease if getting false triggers

docs.title: Visual Feedback
docs.text:
• Enable/disable web interface highlighting
  • Shows which key is pressed
  • Helpful for debugging

docs.title: Session Tracking
docs.text:
The Touch Piano tracks playing sessions:

  • Session start/end times
  • Key press counts per key
  • Total play duration
  • Most frequently used keys

View statistics in the plugin interface.

docs.title: Activity 1: Circuit Discovery
docs.text:
Objective: Identify conductive materials

  1. Gather various objects (pencil, eraser, coin, foil, etc.)
  2. Test each with the piano
  3. Sort into "conductive" and "non-conductive" piles
  4. Discuss why some work and others don't

docs.title: Activity 2: Build a Banana Piano
docs.text:
Objective: Create a working musical instrument

  1. Connect 6 bananas to GPIO pins
  2. Test each banana-key
  3. Play simple songs (Twinkle Twinkle, etc.)
  4. Experiment with different fruits

docs.title: Activity 3: Copper Tape Art
docs.text:
Objective: Create permanent touch pads

  1. Design piano key shapes on cardboard
  2. Apply copper tape to create touch areas
  3. Connect to GPIO pins
  4. Play and decorate your custom piano

docs.title: Activity 4: Science of Sound
docs.text:
Objective: Understand pitch and frequency

  1. Play each key, note the pitch
  2. Compare high vs low notes
  3. Discuss why C is lower than A
  4. Create simple melodies

docs.title: No Sound
docs.text:
1. Check speaker connection
  2. Verify volume > 0%
  3. Test audio device: speaker-test
  4. Check pygame audio initialization

docs.title: Keys Not Responding
docs.text:
1. Check wire connections
  2. Verify GPIO pin numbers
  3. Test with multimeter
  4. Increase sensitivity in settings

docs.title: Intermittent Touch Detection
docs.text:
1. Check for loose connections
  2. Ensure good contact with material
  3. Increase sensitivity
  4. Use more conductive materials (fruit vs foil)

docs.title: False Triggers
docs.text:
1. Decrease sensitivity
  2. Check for electrical interference
  3. Ensure proper grounding
  4. Add capacitors for filtering (advanced)

docs.title: One Key Not Working
docs.text:
1. Test that specific GPIO pin
  2. Check wire continuity
  3. Verify resistor connection
  4. Swap with working key to isolate issue

docs.title: Material Selection
docs.text:
• Fresh fruit works best (higher moisture content)
  • Avoid very dry materials
  • Test materials before class
  • Have backups ready

docs.title: Setup Tips
docs.text:
1. Label connections: Mark which wire goes to which key
  2. Secure wires: Prevent accidental disconnections
  3. Stable surface: Place materials on flat surface
  4. Common ground: Ensure all users touch ground reference

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
• Use only 3.3V GPIO pins
  • Keep water away from electronics
  • Supervise younger students
  • Don't touch bare wires with wet hands

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
• Check connections before each use
  • Replace worn wires
  • Clean contacts if using metal materials
  • Update materials (fruit gets old!)

docs.title: Custom Key Mappings
docs.text:
Edit plugin.py to change notes:

notes = {
    'key_1': 'C4',
    'key_2': 'D4',
    'key_3': 'E4',
    'key_4': 'F4',
    'key_5': 'G4',
    'key_6': 'A4',
}

docs.title: Adding More Keys
docs.text: Requires hardware modification and code changes. See plugin development guide.

docs.title: MIDI Output
docs.text: Can be configured to output MIDI signals for external synthesizers.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• Audio: pygame.midi for synthesis
  • GPIO: gpiozero for input detection
  • Sampling: 60Hz touch detection
  • Debounce: 50ms to prevent multiple triggers
  • Auto-cleanup: Stuck notes timeout after 5 seconds

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• GPIO Wiring - Detailed hardware setup
  • Dashboard - Access the piano
  • Settings - Configure piano options
  • Plugin Development - Customize the piano
